channel.title: Blogs on Tyler Burleigh
channel.description: Recent content in Blogs on Tyler Burleigh

item.title: Looking back at the Data Science Specialization
item.description: I’ve just completed the Data Science Specialization on Coursera, earning my certificate. I decided to take this specialization because I’d just become a data scientist (technically a “Quantitative Researcher”), but I didn’t know what “data science” was. Was it just applied statistics repackaged for industry? At the very least, I figured I’d learn the sorts of terms that data scientists used by convention to refer to things, and I’d be better able to communicate with other data scientists.

item.title: Creating a withdraw button in Qualtrics
item.description: When conducting survey research online using MTurk, researchers are under an ethical obligation to give participants the ability to withdraw from a study without incurring a financial penalty. In practice, however, this is often not the case. In most of the academic Qualtrics surveys I’ve seen, researchers tell participants something to the effect of: “you can withdraw at any time by exiting the survey (e.g., by closing your web browser or navigating away from the survey)”.

item.title: Surviving the Titanic with R caret
item.description: Introduction I’ve been meaning to “upskill” into the world of machine learning for some time. I’m no stranger to statistics. I was trained as a behavioral scientist; I know my way around a linear regression! But at the same time, I tend to use statistics to test formal hypotheses, and this seems (at least to an outsider like me) pretty different from machine learning. I want to learn new skills that will help me be a better data scientist, and machine learning seemes like a good place to start.
